title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: This PR introduces two new endpoints (POST /refunds and GET /customers/:id/transactions) but contains several blocking issues: a silent amount-capping bug that can corrupt refund amounts without signalling an error, a module-level singleton store that contaminates tests and leaks state across app instances, a broken sort comparator (wrong field name hidden behind `any`), a potential full card-number exposure in the history response, and no authorization guard on either new endpoint. Several quality issues round it out: a 200 status for not-found, no double-refund prevention, no input bounds on amount, missing pagination, N+1 refund look-ups, raw console.log bypassing the structured logger, a dead exported schema, and tests with near-vacuous assertions.

findings.title: Potential full card number exposed in history response
findings.message: `(tx as unknown as { card_number?: string }).card_number` escapes the type system to read a `card_number` property that presumably holds a full PAN. If that property ever exists on a stored transaction, the complete card number would be returned in the API response. Even if it doesn't exist today, the pattern signals the field may be stored somewhere and is being deliberately surfaced. Remove the `card_number` field from the response entirely (the `card_last4` field already provides the safe truncated value) and delete this cast.

findings.title: No authentication or authorization on both new endpoints
findings.message: `POST /refunds` and `GET /customers/:id/transactions` are exposed with no auth middleware. Any unauthenticated caller can create refunds for arbitrary transactions or enumerate any customer's transaction history by guessing IDs (IDOR). Add an auth middleware (matching however `/charge` and `/transactions/:id` are protected) and verify the caller is allowed to act on the given `customerId` / `transactionId`.

findings.title: Module-level refundsStore is a process-wide singleton — leaks across tests and app instances
findings.message: `const refundsStore: Record<string, RefundResult> = {}` is declared at module scope, so every `RefundsService` instance created by every `createApp()` call in the same process shares the same store. This is inconsistent with `InMemoryTransactionRepository`, which is constructed fresh per `createApp()`. Concretely, test cases bleed refunds into each other, and in any multi-tenant or test-parallel scenario data leaks between tenants. Move `refundsStore` inside the `RefundsService` class as a private instance field: `private readonly refundsStore: Record<string, RefundResult> = {}`.

findings.title: Silent amount capping silently processes wrong refund amounts instead of rejecting them
findings.message: When `refundAmount > tx.amount` the code tries to detect whether the caller sent dollars instead of cents, and if neither interpretation fits it silently caps the refund at `tx.amount`. The caller receives no error — they requested `amount: 1001` on a `tx.amount: 1000` transaction and get back a `succeeded` refund for `1000` with no indication their input was modified. This is a financial-correctness bug. Remove the unit-detection heuristic entirely (pick one canonical unit and document it), and return a validation error (e.g. 422) when `refundAmount > tx.amount`.

findings.title: Sort comparator uses snake_case `created_at` but Transaction domain uses camelCase `createdAt` — sort is silently broken
findings.message: The `Transaction` domain object stores the timestamp as `createdAt`. The comparator casts to `any` and reads `a.created_at`, which will always be `undefined`, making the sort a no-op and returning transactions in arbitrary insertion order rather than newest-first. Fix: use `a.createdAt` and `b.createdAt` with proper TypeScript types instead of `any`. The `as any` cast is what hid this bug — remove it.

findings.title: Returns HTTP 200 with empty body when transaction is not found — should be 404
findings.message: Returning `200 {}` for a non-existent transaction is misleading and will cause client-side bugs (callers will treat the request as successful). Return `404` with a descriptive error body consistent with the rest of the API. Consider also whether `null` from the service is the right signal — an explicit `TransactionNotFoundError` thrown from the service and caught by the error handler would be cleaner.

findings.title: No double-refund guard — a transaction can be refunded multiple times
findings.message: `refund()` does not check whether the sum of existing refunds plus the new one exceeds `tx.amount`. Calling `POST /refunds` twice for the same transaction will create two succeeded refunds that together exceed the original charge. Add a check: fetch existing refunds via `listRefundsForTransaction`, sum their amounts, and reject if `existingTotal + refundAmount > tx.amount`.

findings.title: Amount field has no lower-bound validation — zero and negative refunds are accepted
findings.message: `z.number().optional()` allows `0`, `-1`, `-999`, etc. A zero-amount refund succeeds and a negative amount would reduce `refund_total`. Change to `z.number().positive().optional()` (or `.min(1)` if amounts are integers in cents) to reject these at the boundary.

findings.title: findAll() returns unbounded set; customerHistoryController scans all transactions to filter by customer
findings.message: `findAll()` returns every transaction in the store, and the controller then filters in a loop. This is O(total transactions) per request. Even with the current in-memory store, this grows without bound. Add a `findByCustomerId(customerId: string)` method to the repository interface so the repository can index lookups, and add a `limit`/`cursor` parameter so the history endpoint can paginate results.

findings.title: N+1 sequential refund lookups inside transaction loop
findings.message: For each transaction `tx`, `enrichTransactionWithRefunds` calls `listRefundsForTransaction` which performs a full linear scan of `refundsStore`. This is O(n × m) where n = customer transactions and m = total refunds. Even in-memory this is wasteful. Fetch all refunds once (or index by `transactionId`) and do a single-pass join.

findings.title: Tests assert almost nothing — all would pass even if endpoints return error responses
findings.message:
• `expect(refund.body).toBeDefined()` is always true. Assert `refund.status` is `201` and check required fields (`refund_id`, `transaction_id`, `amount`, `status`).
• `expect(refund.status).toBeLessThan(500)` accepts `404` or `422`, making the test vacuous.
• The history test only asserts the body is defined — check `res.status === 200` and the response shape.
• Missing test cases: partial refund, double-refund rejection, negative/zero amount, history populated after a charge.

findings.title: historyQuerySchema is defined but never used
findings.message: `historyQuerySchema` is exported but never imported or applied in `customerHistoryController.ts` or `routes.ts`. Either wire it up to validate query parameters on the history route (e.g. `limit`, `cursor`) or remove it.

findings.title: Raw console.log used instead of the structured pino logger
findings.message: The app uses `pino-http` for structured JSON logging. `console.log` in controllers and services bypasses correlation IDs, log levels, and structured fields — lines like `'history requested for customer ' + customerId` and the similar ones in `refundsService.ts` will be noisy and unsearchable in production. Thread the pino logger instance through (or use `req.log`) and replace all `console.log` calls.

findings.title: Loose equality `==` instead of strict `===` when comparing customerId
findings.message: `tx.customerId == customerId` uses abstract equality. Both values are strings, so this works today, but `===` is the consistent standard throughout the codebase and avoids unexpected coercion if types ever change. Change to `===`.

findings.title: Unused `_reason` parameter — dead code
findings.message: `_reason` is accepted by `refund()` and forwarded from the controller/schema, but is never stored on the `RefundResult` or used in any way. Either store it in the refund record (useful for audit trails) or remove it from the service signature, schema, and controller call.
